case_name: M/S. MANDVI CO-OP BANK LTD. ..........Appellant VERSUS NIMESH B. THAKORE ..........Respondent
facts: These appeals arise from multiple High Court writ petitions concerning the procedure for dishonoured-cheque trials under Chapter XVII of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 after the 2002 amendments (sections 143-147). The High Court had framed questions whether section 145(2) confers an unfettered right to require oral examination-in-chief of persons who gave evidence on affidavit and whether the 2002 amendments applied to pending complaints. The disputes include whether the accused may insist that a complainant or his witness who has filed an affidavit must depose viva voce in examination-in-chief and whether the accused may give evidence on affidavit. The Supreme Court heard seven appeals arising out of SLP (Crl.) matters including SLP (Crl.) No.3915/2006 and others.
arguments_claimant: The appellant(s) (banks) argued for a literal construction of section 145(2), submitting that the word "examine" should be read to include examination-in-chief, cross-examination and re-examination, and that where the accused applies the court is obliged to call the deponent and examine him. They relied on textual/literal interpretation authorities (e.g. Dental Council, Raghunath Rai Bareja) and urged that the provision mirrors section 296(2) CrPC so should be read similarly. They also contended that the accused should not be permitted to delay proceedings by refusing to accept affidavit-evidence.
arguments_respondent: The respondents (and the High Court view supported by some parties) maintained that section 145 must be read in light of the scheme of sections 143-146 to ensure speedy summary procedure and that the affidavit already on record is "obviously in the nature of examination-in-chief." They argued the accused's right under section 145(2) is limited to having the deponent summoned for cross-examination and that allowing the accused to insist on redoing examination-in-chief would lead to needless duplication and delay. The High Court also held the 2002 amendments are procedural and apply to pending cases.
ratio_decidendi: "What section 145(2) of the Act says is simply this. The court may, at its discretion, call a person giving his evidence on affidavit and examine him as to the facts contained therein. But if an application is made either by the prosecution or by the accused the court must call the person giving his evidence on affidavit, again to be examined as to the facts contained therein. The scheme of sections 143 to 146 does not in any way affect the judge's powers under section 165 of the Evidence Act. The affidavit of the person so summoned that is already on the record is obviously in the nature of examination-in-chief. Hence, on being summoned on the application made by the accused the deponent of the affidavit (the complainant or any of his witnesses) can only be subjected to cross-examination as to the facts stated in the affidavit. Any other meaning given to sub-section (2) of section 145 ... would make the provision of section 145(1) nugatory and would completely defeat the very scheme of trial as designed under sections 143 to 147."
final_order: The Court set aside the High Court's direction permitting the accused to tender his evidence on affidavit (appeal arising from SLP (Crl.) No.3915/2006 allowed) and dismissed the remaining six appeals. There shall be no order as to costs.
summary: This Supreme Court judgment (2010) decides the scope of section 145 of the Negotiable Instruments Act (2002 amendments) regarding affidavit-evidence in dishonoured-cheque trials. The Court held that an affidavit filed by the complainant is in the nature of examination-in-chief and, when summoned at the instance of the accused under section 145(2), the deponent can only be subjected to cross-examination on the affidavit's contents; the accused cannot compel a fresh oral examination-in-chief. The Court also held sections 143-147 are procedural and apply to pending cases, but disallowed the High Court's reading that the accused may always give evidence on affidavit.
embedding_summary: M/S. Mandvi Co-Op Bank Ltd v. Nimesh B. Thakore, Supreme Court of India, 11 January 2010, AIR 2010 SC 1402, 2010 (3) SCC 83 (Criminal Appeal arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.3915/2006 and others). Issue: scope of section 145 Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (as amended 2002) for dishonoured-cheque (section 138) trials — whether affidavit evidence can be re-opened as oral examination-in-chief on summons and whether amendments apply to pending cases. Key statutes: sections 143-147 Negotiable Instruments Act 1881 (2002 amendment), section 138 NI Act, section 165 Evidence Act, section 137 Evidence Act, section 296(2) CrPC, sections 315/316 CrPC, Article 20(3) Constitution. Holding: affidavit of complainant is "obviously in the nature of examination-in-chief," the deponent when summoned at accused's instance can be subjected only to cross-examination on affidavit contents; sections 143-147 are procedural and apply retrospectively; High Court direction allowing accused to give evidence on affidavit was set aside (one appeal allowed; six appeals dismissed). No damages or compensation issues.
legal_principles: Affidavit evidence of the complainant is "obviously in the nature of examination-in-chief"; on being summoned by the accused the deponent can only be cross-examined as to facts in the affidavit
The court "may" summon on its discretion but "shall" summon on application by prosecution or accused; extent of examination follows scheme of sections 143-146 and Evidence Act powers (s.165)
Sections 143-147 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 2002 are procedural in nature and apply to cases pending when the amendments came into force
Prosecution may summon a witness who gave affidavit-evidence for re-examination or to cure defects in proof of documents

A court must not, by interpretation, read into section 145(1) a right for the accused to give evidence on affidavit where the legislature omitted it (judicial restraint against usurping legislative function)
sections_cited: Section 165 of the Evidence Act (Indian Evidence Act)
Section 137 of the Indian Evidence Act
Section 296(2) of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973
Sections 315 and 316 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973
Sections 262 to 265 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (referenced)
Article 20(3) of the Constitution of India
cases_cited: Dental Council of India vs. Hari Prakash and Ors., (2001) 8 SCC 61
Nathi Devi vs. Radha Devi, (2005) 2 SCC 271
Raghunath Rai Bareja and Anr. vs. Punjab National Bank and Ors., (2007) 2 SCC 230
State of Punjab vs. Naib Din, (2001) 8 SCC 578
Gurbachan Singh vs. Satpal Singh and Ors., 1990 (1) SCC 445
Union of India and Anr. vs. Deoki Nandan Aggarwal, 1992 Supp. (1) SCC 323
Duport Steels Ltd. vs. Sirs, [1980] 1 All ER 529
bm25_text: M/S. MANDVI CO-OP BANK LTD. ..........Appellant VERSUS NIMESH B. THAKORE ..........Respondent THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 2010 These appeals arise from multiple High Court writ petitions concerning the procedure for dishonoured-cheque trials under Chapter XVII of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 after the 2002 amendments (sections 143-147). The High Court had framed questions whether section 145(2) confers an unfettered right to require oral examination-in-chief of persons who gave evidence on affidavit and whether the 2002 amendments applied to pending complaints. The disputes include whether the accused may insist that a complainant or his witness who has filed an affidavit must depose viva voce in examination-in-chief and whether the accused may give evidence on affidavit. The Supreme Court heard seven appeals arising out of SLP (Crl.) matters including SLP (Crl.) No.3915/2006 and others. The appellant(s) (banks) argued for a literal construction of section 145(2), submitting that the word "examine" should be read to include examination-in-chief, cross-examination and re-examination, and that where the accused applies the court is obliged to call the deponent and examine him. They relied on textual/literal interpretation authorities (e.g. Dental Council, Raghunath Rai Bareja) and urged that the provision mirrors section 296(2) CrPC so should be read similarly. They also contended that the accused should not be permitted to delay proceedings by refusing to accept affidavit-evidence. The respondents (and the High Court view supported by some parties) maintained that section 145 must be read in light of the scheme of sections 143-146 to ensure speedy summary procedure and that the affidavit already on record is "obviously in the nature of examination-in-chief." They argued the accused's right under section 145(2) is limited to having the deponent summoned for cross-examination and that allowing the accused to insist on redoing examination-in-chief would lead to needless duplication and delay. The High Court also held the 2002 amendments are procedural and apply to pending cases. "What section 145(2) of the Act says is simply this. The court may, at its discretion, call a person giving his evidence on affidavit and examine him as to the facts contained therein. But if an application is made either by the prosecution or by the accused the court must call the person giving his evidence on affidavit, again to be examined as to the facts contained therein. The scheme of sections 143 to 146 does not in any way affect the judge's powers under section 165 of the Evidence Act. The affidavit of the person so summoned that is already on the record is obviously in the nature of examination-in-chief. Hence, on being summoned on the application made by the accused the deponent of the affidavit (the complainant or any of his witnesses) can only be subjected to cross-examination as to the facts stated in the affidavit. Any other meaning given to sub-section (2) of section 145 ... would make the provision of section 145(1) nugatory and would completely defeat the very scheme of trial as designed under sections 143 to 147." The Court set aside the High Court's direction permitting the accused to tender his evidence on affidavit (appeal arising from SLP (Crl.) No.3915/2006 allowed) and dismissed the remaining six appeals. There shall be no order as to costs. This Supreme Court judgment (2010) decides the scope of section 145 of the Negotiable Instruments Act (2002 amendments) regarding affidavit-evidence in dishonoured-cheque trials. The Court held that an affidavit filed by the complainant is in the nature of examination-in-chief and, when summoned at the instance of the accused under section 145(2), the deponent can only be subjected to cross-examination on the affidavit's contents; the accused cannot compel a fresh oral examination-in-chief. The Court also held sections 143-147 are procedural and apply to pending cases, but disallowed the High Court's reading that the accused may always give evidence on affidavit. M/S. Mandvi Co-Op Bank Ltd v. Nimesh B. Thakore, Supreme Court of India, 11 January 2010, AIR 2010 SC 1402, 2010 (3) SCC 83 (Criminal Appeal arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.3915/2006 and others). Issue: scope of section 145 Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (as amended 2002) for dishonoured-cheque (section 138) trials — whether affidavit evidence can be re-opened as oral examination-in-chief on summons and whether amendments apply to pending cases. Key statutes: sections 143-147 Negotiable Instruments Act 1881 (2002 amendment), section 138 NI Act, section 165 Evidence Act, section 137 Evidence Act, section 296(2) CrPC, sections 315/316 CrPC, Article 20(3) Constitution. Holding: affidavit of complainant is "obviously in the nature of examination-in-chief," the deponent when summoned at accused's instance can be subjected only to cross-examination on affidavit contents; sections 143-147 are procedural and apply retrospectively; High Court direction allowing accused to give evidence on affidavit was set aside (one appeal allowed; six appeals dismissed). No damages or compensation issues.
